title: Egg on Cotton
stainChemistry: Egg is a protein-family stain (egg-white albumin plus a yolk fat / pigment fraction). Protein stains coagulate and set under heat, so the absolute first rule is cold start — no hot water, no steam, no iron, no dryer until the protein is removed. Cotton is cellulose: white cotton is generally tolerant of conservative laundering and enzyme-containing detergents; colored cotton requires lower heat and a colorfastness check before any escalation. This card is scoped to cotton — enzyme / protein chemistry is not safe on protein fibers (silk, wool) and should not be generalized.
whyThisWorks: A cold rinse keeps the egg-white protein soluble and carries away the bulk of the stain before any heat can set it. An enzyme-containing laundry detergent (per its own product label and used at the label's specified temperature) breaks down protein bonds in the residue. Care-label laundering and air-drying with inspection prevent any residual yolk pigment or protein from being heat-set into the fiber.
defaultAssumption: Most egg on cotton is a kitchen / breakfast stain on a washable garment. Fresh raw egg responds to a cold rinse, an enzyme-containing detergent applied per its product label, and a care-label wash. Egg that has been through warm or hot water, an iron, or a dryer before treatment is meaningfully harder and may need a professional dry cleaner.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Confirm fiber, color, and care-label limits. Dry-clean-only or structured pieces escalate to the professional path. Confirm no heat (hot water, iron, dryer) has touched the stain yet.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Reject too-soiled or non-colorfast garments per care-label guidance. Do not proceed if the care label disallows wet treatment.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Use a dull edge to gently lift any solid egg or yolk off the fabric. Do not rub or press into the fiber.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Minimal mechanical action only. Aggressive rubbing can spread the stain or push protein into the fiber.

spottingProtocol.agent: Cold water rinse from the back
spottingProtocol.instruction: Hold the back of the stained area under cold running water and let the rinse carry the egg out through the front. Blot underneath with a clean white cotton cloth — do not rub.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Cold water only. Warm or hot water coagulates egg protein and sets the stain into the fiber.

spottingProtocol.instruction: On colored cotton, dab a small amount of laundry detergent and cool water on an inside seam using a clean white cloth. Press for one minute and check the cloth for color transfer.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Stop if dye transfers. Colored or unstable-dye garments escalate to the professional path.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply an enzyme-containing laundry detergent (per its product label) directly to the stain at the temperature specified on the product label. Work it in lightly with a fingertip or soft cloth.
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: Per product-label dwell time; do not exceed
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Follow the product label exactly. Do not exceed the product-label temperature; high heat denatures the enzyme. Do not mix cleaning agents.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Launder using the temperature setting permitted by the care label; favor cool or lukewarm when uncertain. Rinse thoroughly.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Do not exceed the temperature limit on the care label.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Air-dry the garment and inspect for any residual stain or yolk shadow before any dryer heat. If a stain remains, repeat the cold rinse and enzyme pretreat once; do not machine-dry until the stain is fully gone.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Heat applied to residual protein can coagulate it permanently into the fiber.

steps: 1. Identify fiber, dye stability, and care-label limits before any wet treatment; confirm no heat has touched the stain yet.
2. Lift excess egg with a dull edge; minimal mechanical action.
3. Cold rinse from the back of the fabric.
4. Apply a pro protein-digester appropriate to the protein family, per product SDS / label; rinse and assess.
5. Launder per care-label temperature limit; do not exceed.

6. Air-dry and inspect before any heat. If a residual yolk pigment shadow remains, apply controlled oxidizer only when the care label permits, colorfastness is confirmed, and PPE / ventilation per product SDS are in place.
products: Enzyme-containing laundry detergent (consumer-grade) for the Home path
Cool / lukewarm water per care label
professionalProtocol.temperature: Cold rinse; care-label cool / lukewarm wash; no heat on residual protein
warnings: Never use hot water, steam, an iron, or a dryer on egg until the protein has been removed
Never use chlorine bleach as a protein treatment
Confirm colorfastness via hidden-area test before any escalation
Do not exceed the enzyme product-label temperature; high heat denatures the enzyme
Enzymes / protein digesters are not safe on protein fibers (silk, wool) — this card is cotton-only
Pro chemistry beyond consumer enzyme detergent requires SDS-driven PPE and ventilation

homeSolutions.agent: Confirm Care Label — Cold Only From Here
homeSolutions.instruction: Check the care label. If it says dry-clean-only, or the garment is structured / lined / embellished / vintage, stop and take it to a professional dry cleaner. For washable cotton, continue. Keep everything cold until the egg is out — warm or hot water cooks the egg protein into the fiber permanently.
Use a dull butter knife or spoon edge to gently lift any solid egg or yolk off the fabric. Do not rub — that pushes the protein into the fiber.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water Rinse from the Back
homeSolutions.instruction: Flip the fabric inside out and hold the back of the stained area under cold running water for 30–60 seconds. Most fresh egg rinses out at this step. Blot underneath with a clean white cloth — do not rub.

On colored cotton, dab a little laundry detergent and cool water on an inside seam with a clean white cloth. Press one minute. If color transfers to the cloth, stop and take to a professional. White cotton can skip this step.

Apply an enzyme-containing laundry detergent directly to the stain per its product label (use the temperature the product label specifies — usually cool or lukewarm). Work it in gently with your fingertip or a soft cloth. Do not exceed the product label's recommended temperature — hot water destroys the enzyme.
Wash the garment using the temperature your care label allows. If you are unsure, use cool or lukewarm water. Rinse thoroughly.

homeSolutions.agent: Air-Dry and Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Air-dry the garment and look closely for any remaining mark before putting it in the dryer. If a stain remains, repeat the cold rinse and enzyme pretreat once. Do not machine-dry until the stain is fully gone — heat sets any residual protein into cotton permanently.

If the stain is still there after two careful passes, or if the egg has already been through warm / hot water, an iron, or a dryer, take the garment to a professional dry cleaner. Tell them: "Egg on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with cold rinse, enzyme-containing laundry detergent, and a care-label wash. [Any heat applied before treatment? — yes / no]."

steps: 1. Read care label; escalate dry-clean-only / structured / vintage to a pro. Keep everything cold until the egg is out.
2. Lift excess egg gently with a dull edge — do not rub.
3. Cold water rinse from the BACK of the fabric.
4. On colored cotton, hidden-area colorfastness test before detergent.
5. Apply enzyme-containing laundry detergent per product label (do not exceed product-label temperature).
6. Launder per care label (favor cool / lukewarm if uncertain).
7. Air-dry and inspect for any residual mark before any dryer heat.
8. Repeat once if needed; escalate if it doesn't clear or any heat was applied before treatment.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the care label is dry-clean-only, the garment is structured / lined / embellished, the hidden-area test shows color transfer, the egg has already been through warm / hot water / an iron / a dryer, or two conservative passes do not clear it. In any of those cases, escalate to a professional dry cleaner.
warnings: Do not use warm or hot water on raw egg — protein coagulates and sets the stain
Do not use a steam, iron, or dryer until the protein is fully out
Do not exceed the enzyme product-label temperature
Do not rub or scrub — pushes protein into the fiber
Do not use chlorine bleach as a protein treatment
Enzyme protein chemistry is cotton-only on this card — not for silk / wool
Pro-strength oxidizers, solvents, and protein digesters are not Home steps

neverDo: Never use warm or hot water on raw egg — protein coagulates and sets the stain
Never apply steam, an iron, or a dryer to egg before the protein is removed
Never use chlorine bleach as a protein treatment
Never exceed the enzyme product-label temperature; high heat denatures the enzyme
Never apply enzymes / protein digesters to silk or wool (this card is cotton-only)
Never apply professional-strength oxidizers or solvents in the Home path
fiberSensitivities: Cotton is cellulose; white cotton is generally tolerant of conservative laundering and enzyme-containing detergents
Colored cotton requires lower heat and a colorfastness check before escalation
Heat-set or aged egg residue is harder to remove and may need a professional dry cleaner
chemicalBoundaries: Home path: cold rinse plus an enzyme-containing or mild liquid laundry detergent used per its product label
No peroxide, oxygen bleach, or chlorine bleach as a default Home step

Pro path may use controlled protein-digester chemistry or, only for a residual yolk pigment shadow, controlled oxidizer chemistry when care label permits, colorfastness is confirmed, and SDS-driven PPE / ventilation are in place
homeAllowed: Enzyme-containing or mild liquid laundry detergent, used per product label
Cold water for rinse; cool / lukewarm wash water per care label
Clean white cotton cloth and dull edge for gentle mechanical lift
Air-drying and visual inspection before any heat
proOnly: Pro protein-digester formulas and oxidizers used in a controlled professional environment with PPE / ventilation per product SDS
testFirst: Hidden-area colorfastness test (white cloth on an inside seam) before detergent dwell or any escalation on colored cotton
flushNeutralizeDry: Rinse thoroughly with cold water from the back of the fabric
Air-dry and inspect before any dryer heat
escalationTriggers: Care label is dry-clean-only or restricts wet treatment
Garment is structured / lined / embellished / vintage / high-value
Stain has been through warm / hot water, an iron, or a dryer before treatment
Two conservative passes do not clear the stain
ppeVentilation: Follow the product label for any concentrate
Gloves and eye protection for any pro-strength chemistry
Ventilate the work area; keep cleaning agents away from children and pets
safetyMatrix.homeVsProBoundary: Home users use cold rinse plus an enzyme-containing or mild liquid laundry detergent used per product label, with a care-label wash. Pro chemistry (concentrated protein digesters, oxidizers) is hidden from the Home path and requires SDS-driven PPE / ventilation in a controlled environment.

materialWarnings: Cold only on raw egg — warm or hot water sets the protein
No iron / no steam / no dryer until the protein is out
Enzymes are cotton-safe on this card — not for silk / wool
Inspect for any residual mark before any dryer heat

escalation.when: Egg has been through warm / hot water, an iron, or a dryer before treatment; dry-clean-only, structured, or vintage cotton; colored cotton that fails a hidden-area test; or no improvement after two conservative passes.
escalation.whatToTell: "Egg on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with cold rinse, enzyme-containing laundry detergent, and a care-label wash. [Any heat applied before treatment? — yes / no]."

contextQuestions: Has any warm / hot water, iron, or dryer touched the stain since the spill?
Raw egg or cooked egg residue?
How old is the stain — fresh, overnight, or older?
White, light, or dark / dyed cotton?
Is the garment dry-clean-only, structured, or embellished?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Egg is a protein stain on cotton — the absolute rule is cold start until the protein is removed. Enzyme-containing laundry detergent (per product label) handles fresh raw egg on cotton; the card does not generalize that chemistry to silk or wool, where protein digesters are unsafe. Heat-set egg is meaningfully harder and should be escalated to a professional dry cleaner rather than treated with stronger Home chemistry.

sources.title: University Stain Science (compiled extension reference)
sources.evidence: Egg is a protein stain; cold water / enzyme presoak / warm wash sequence; hot water can cook / set protein; chlorine bleach warning for protein stains; enzymes are unsafe on silk / wool; blot rather than rub; do not iron or dry stained fabric until removal is confirmed.

sources.title: EPA Alternative Cleaning Curriculum
sources.evidence: Soil classes including water-soluble and combination soils; surfactant / enzyme principles; protein-remover use for blood and egg; test new detergents on inconspicuous areas; follow product labels and PPE / ventilation for concentrates; clean per care label or reject inappropriate garments.

sources.title: GONR Material Safety Matrix v2
sources.evidence: Cotton fiber tolerances; protease enzymes break protein bonds and are used for egg, safe on cotton / cellulose / synthetics but never on protein fibers; hot water denatures enzymes; oxidizer safety boundaries (white / colorfast / care-label only); protein formula used for egg / sweat; absolute safety gates including no hot water on protein stains and no cleaner-mixing.

sources.title: BÜFA Professional Spotting Guide
sources.evidence: Fresh protein is water-soluble; old / coagulated protein needs enzymatic treatment; protein denatures around 40°C; older protein may need enzymes.
